methodology: Articles sourced from Virginia-specific RSS feeds and Google News RSS, filtered for AI/technology/workforce relevance, then assessed by Claude against the scenario framework. Each article is tagged with the region it most affects and the scenario it signals toward.

signals.title: New partnership between VCU and AI Ready RVA aims to boost AI education, innovation and community impact - VCU News
signals.signal_reading: VCU's partnership with AI Ready RVA signals intentional knowledge infrastructure building beyond NoVA, creating peer-to-peer learning networks that could distribute deep AI capability across Richmond's diverse community sectors.

signals.title: Report says 35% of the jobs in Virginia may be at risk to artificial intelligence. Southside the least impacted, Northern Virginia the most. - cardinalnews.org
signals.signal_reading: The 35% job displacement risk concentrated in NoVA while Southside faces least impact reveals a paradox: regions with AI infrastructure face disruption without guaranteed capability transfer, while rural areas remain structurally excluded from both threat and opportunity.

signals.title: EPA grant backs community-driven workforce training in Southwest Virginia - Virginia Tech News
signals.signal_reading: EPA-funded community-driven workforce training in Southwest Virginia represents grassroots capacity building that could seed distributed AI fluency in coal transition regions, though success depends on whether training reaches deep technical skills or shallow platform adoption.

signals.title: AI is here in Hampton Roads, and businesses and organizations are embracing it - The Virginian-Pilot
signals.signal_reading: Hampton Roads businesses embracing AI suggests platform-enabled adoption spreading beyond NoVA, but the nature of 'embracing' determines whether this creates shallow tool usage or deeper regional capability in the defense-tech corridor.

signals.title: AI trainer says workers must lead technology, not follow it - WDBJ7
signals.signal_reading: AI trainer emphasizing worker leadership over passive technology adoption signals pedagogy for deep capability building in Southwest Virginia, directly countering 'software does it for you' narratives that concentrate power in platform providers.
